content:
Practical guidance for “Can and jar safety shelf” within “Food · Agriculture · Preservation”. It supports safe storage, temperature control, preparation, rationing, and spoilage decisions, produces a visible result, and states when to stop or move to a safer fallback.

Start with: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. This is the opening check for “Can and jar safety shelf” in “Food · Agriculture · Preservation”.

Stop condition: Do not use unknown plants, contaminated water, untreated sewage, unidentified chemicals, or diseased material in food production. Apply this boundary to “Can and jar safety shelf” in “Food · Agriculture · Preservation”.

Reduced fallback: If “Can and jar safety shelf” cannot be completed, do only the first verified step, mark the remaining capability unavailable, and use a shelf-stable meal prepared with safe water and clean utensils.

Identify crop or animal needs, season, soil, water quality, exposure, labour, and the smallest useful trial. Record the result under “Food · Agriculture · Preservation” before continuing “Can and jar safety shelf”.

Keep clean stock, seed, feed, harvest, compost, manure, chemicals, and diseased material separated and labelled. Recheck this point before closing “Can and jar safety shelf” for “Food · Agriculture · Preservation”.
Use observation rows or small test plots before changing the whole system. Treat any uncertainty here as an unresolved item in “Can and jar safety shelf” for “Food · Agriculture · Preservation”.
Record weather, watering, inputs, pests, disease signs, growth, yield, losses, and labour. A second person should be able to verify this part of “Can and jar safety shelf”.

Save diverse healthy seed or breeding stock only from known sources and revise the next cycle from measured results. Include the outcome of “Can and jar safety shelf” in the handover for “Food · Agriculture · Preservation”.

This card is not authorization to taste questionable food to test it, hide spoilage with cooking, or serve an unknown ingredient to someone with a serious allergy.
